observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC - Municipal Treasurer to:
Direct the Revenue Collectors to ensure that collections made for the current day are strictly remitted to the OIC - Municipal Treasurer on a daily basis and require them to accomplish the RCDs at the close of each business day;
Require the Liquidating Officer to thoroughly verify all collections turned over to her and accomplish the RCD.
The OIC - Municipal Treasurer is to perform verification of RCD, which includes checking the statement of accountable forms, conducting a physical count of the accountable forms and reconciling them with records, counting the cash received to ensure it matches the reported collections, and signing the certification and receipt portions of all RCD copies; and
The OIC - Municipal Treasurer to ensure that deposits are made with the authorized government depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day.
These measures will ensure timely remittance and compliance with established rules and regulations, thereby strengthening the Municipality's internal control system.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to complete the review of the identified reconciling items and effect the necessary journal entries in the books to accurately reflect the correct balances of the Cash in Bank-LCCA account and its corresponding income and expense accounts presented in the Municipality's Financial Statements.
The Municipal Accountant to prepare the monthly bank reconciliation statements and submit a copy to the Audit Team for review within 10 days of receiving the bank statement, in accordance with Paragraph 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996. This process ensures the timely detection of errors or fraudulent transactions, whether committed by the Municipality or the bank, and helps maintain the fair presentation of the Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements.
Adhere to the systematic approach for recording the check disbursements under Chapter 3, Section 44 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs to ensure proper account recording and classification, thereby enhancing the reliability and accuracy of the Financial Statements.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Designate a team or employees within the Municipal Treasury Office through a memorandum. This team will be responsible for preparing, maintaining, and updating the certified list of taxpayers, which should include their names, property descriptions, periods of arrears, and the amounts due based on the Real Property Tax Account Register and the taxpayer index card.
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the certified list of taxpayers. This will ensure an accurate and reliable basis for establishing receivables for Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special Education Tax (SET) at the beginning of the year, thus guaranteeing the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
The Treasury Office to:
Continue conducting an information drive and tax campaign to raise the awareness of its constituents regarding the advance payment of the real property taxes and to avail the discounts, encouraging taxpayers to pay promptly and improving collection efficiency of the Municipality on RPT/SET; and
Continue sending letters to delinquent taxpayers and post notice of delinquency at the main entrance of the municipal hall and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each Barangay of the local government unit concerned. Also, the Municipality may apply the available legal remedies to generate more revenue that may be appropriated for the implementation of additional programs and projects for the benefit of its constituents.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify semi-expendable property that was incorrectly recorded under the Property, Plant and Equipment as follows:
Issued to end-users
Refer to Annex C of COA Circular No. 2022-004 as a guide in preparing adjustments for prior-year acquisitions of property and equipment below the ₱50,000 capitalization threshold that were improperly charged to PPE or other inventory accounts, if any.
To enhance accountability and internal control over semi-expendable property, the Municipal Government shall ensure the proper preparation and maintenance of the following prescribed forms, registries, and reports, as applicable, pursuant to Item 4.7 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022:
Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card (SPLC)
Registry of Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RegSPI)
Receipt of Returned Semi-Expendable Property (RRSP)
Report of Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RSPI)
Report on the Physical Count of Semi-Expendable Property (RPCSP)
Report of Lost, Stolen, Damaged, or Destroyed Semi-Expendable Property (RLSDDSP)
Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Semi-Expendable Property (IIRUSP)
These measures will ensure proper recording, monitoring, and accountability over semi-expendable property, reducing the risk of loss, misuse, or misstatement in the Municipality’s financial reports.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 be complied with.
Further, we have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
The Municipal Accountant is to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. He is further requested to cooperate with the Audit Team to ensure that issues are thoroughly discussed with the intention of arriving at a feasible solution, and
The Municipal Property Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keeps a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and their components.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to provide the details of the breakdown of Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund as disclosures in the Note to Financial Statements, as required by IPSAS 1.
Management to adhere to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 mandating LGUs that at year-end all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) and the Disaster Risk Management Fund for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (DRRMF-MOOE) shall be transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the account "Trust Liability-DRRM" (Code 2-04-01-020) in the Trust Fund Books.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to provide the necessary disclosures and comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS and the Manual on NGAS for LGUs by adequately inserting a concise narrative on significant changes and a breakdown of material balances to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the Financial Statements. The Accountant should also trace the details of the differences arising from the reconciliation of SCBAA and SFPer under Income, MOOE, and Personal Services.
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to maintain RAAOs in support of the actual amounts in the SCBAA and the reconciliation with the amounts presented in the SFPer.

We have recommended that the Management require the Local School Board to:
Provide a copy of the Approved SEF Budget to the Municipal Accountant within seven days after the approval of the SEF Budget;
Require the submission to be within seven days after the approval of the SEF Budget and to indicate the date of receipt to determine the timeliness of the LSB's submission.
Submit quarterly and annual reports to the DepEd CO, with copies provided to the local sanggunian and the Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG within the prescribed deadlines and
Adhere to the posting requirements for the SEF Utilization Reports as stated in Section 6.1 of DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017

We have recommended that Management:
Ensure that an updated GAD Database is maintained to facilitate the identification of gender issues and guarantee the full implementation of the Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) outlined in the GAD Plan and Budget.

Additionally, the PPAs that do not address gender-related concerns due to ambiguity should be charged to MOOE instead of the fund designated for GAD.

Changes between the original and final budgets;



Material discrepancies between the budget and actual amounts; and

Reconciliation of actual amounts per SCBAA and per Statement of Financial Performance, to enhance the transparency of the Financial Statements of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Municipal Engineer to

observations.managementAction:
AAR 2022 –p79 | In a letter dated April 26, 2023, the Municipal Engineer clarified that the closure of the sanitary landfill was required by the DENR due to its previous operation as a controlled dumpsite


The sanitary landfill serves as a temporary residual waste facility for those wastes that can no longer be recycled where the truck owned by the private contractor collects and delivers it to their disposal site. Also, the site was used for a brick-making facility and contained machinery such as a granulator and constructed a leachate pond as preparation for the proposed Sanitary Landfill.

Management assured that they will continue to comply with the requirements and procedures on proper waste disposal of the DENR-EMB.
observations.reason:
No copy of the order from DENR-EMB was submitted to the audit team.





Implemented

The site was not functioning as a Sanitary Landfill but rather as a Temporary Residual Containment Facility and an Area Material Recovery Facility. A Leachate pond was also built in preparation for the Proposed Sanitary Landfill and it had Solid waste management equipment in placed. 

Furthermore, the municipality has acquired services from a private contractor. The audit team also received a copy of the MOA.





Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

Instruct the BPLO, being the Office in charge of receiving and processing permits per Section 4 (j and k) of the IRR of RA No. 11032 to prevent uncountersigned and unjustified erasures in the Application Form for Business Permits especially the amount of Declared Gross Sales/ Receipts for the preceding year of business entities as it affects the amount of tax that would be assessed and collected from the taxpayer.  As provided in the Instructions in accomplishing the form, the applicant shall “(1) Provide accurate information and print legibly to avoid delays.  Incomplete application form will be returned to the applicant. (2) Ensure that all documents attached to the form (if any) are complete and properly filled out.”

Require the BPLO and the Municipal Treasurer to secure a Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts or Sales for the preceding year from the operators of business subject to the taxes on business in compliance with Section 2C.04 (d) of the 2005 Revenue Code of the Municipality to ascertain the declared gross sales or receipts of business entities.

Instruct also the BPLO and Municipal Treasurer to require the submission of Certified Income Tax Return Copy from all persons who are granted a permit to conduct an activity or business and who are liable to pay the business tax on or before April 30 of each year pursuant to Section 2C.04 (e)  of the same Revenue Code. The deficiency in the business tax arising out of the difference in gross receipts or sales declared in the application for Mayor’s Permit/ Declaration of gross sales or receipts and the gross receipts or sales declared in the ITR shall be payable on or before May 20 of the same year with interest at the rate of ten percent (10%) corresponding to the two percent (2%) per month from January to May.  Payments of the deficiency tax made after May 20 shall be subject to the twenty-five percent (25%) surcharge and two percent (2%) interest for every month counted from January up to the month payment is made.

The two documents, a) Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts or Sales for the preceding year from the  operators of business and b) the Certified Income Tax Return Copy from all persons who are granted a permit to conduct an activity or business and who are liable to pay the business tax, shall form part of the checklist of requirements in the Citizen’s Charter for application of business permit per Section 4 (q and s) of the IRR of RA No. 11032 to align the documents required under the Revenue Code of the Municipality and the Ease of Doing Business and Efficient Government Service Delivery Act

Instruct the BPLO and the Municipal Treasury to have a repository in its BPLS, the complete record of each person/ entity paying revenue per Section 7A.08. of the Revenue Code which includes among others the Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts or Sales for the preceding year and the Certified Income Tax Return Copy.  The correct nature or line of business shall also be updated in the records and the related Section of the Revenue Code as basis of computation of business tax to avoid confusion and inconsistencies of application.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer as the Accountable Officer to regularly reconcile the PCF Balance properly without resorting to force balancing. 

Compare, check, reconcile and make necessary adjustments on the footings made in Journals/Ledgers of the Municipal Accountant as compared to the Cashbook balance of the Municipal Treasurer to avoid discrepancies that renders the Petty Cash Fund inaccurate.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Accounting Unit to set up the amount of RPT/SET receivables at the beginning of the year and to RPT and SET receivables written off at year-end to present fairly the balances of these accounts in the financial statements pursuant to Section 246 of the Local Government Code and Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to prepare and maintain complete and updated PPE Ledger Cards and property cards, respectively, and require them to reconcile these records regularly.

We reiterated that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be complied. Thus, we recommend Management to require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the General Services Officer or in his absence, the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Engineer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We recommended that that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to require all the employees who were paid COVID-19 hazard pay to submit proof of actual exposure to hazards and the documents required under Item 5.8 of COA No. Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support the payment of their hazard pay.

We recommended the following to Management:

Require the concerned Municipal Office to prepare the Summary/ List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, and be submitted to the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, through the Office of the Civil Defense, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to record any balances of donations received be costed and recorded in the books or in the registries.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Health Officer and/ or Supply and Property Management Officer in coordination with the Municipal Accountant to submit the Delivery Receipt, pictures showing proof of delivery to the Municipality and a Summary List of Distribution List per Barangay of the procured vitamins and medicines specifically the 3500 boxes of Sodium Ascorbate (Vitamin C caps) 500mg caps and 1150 tabs of Azithromycin tabs in compliance with Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 to corroborate completeness of delivery of medicines and vitamins and distribution of the same to the intended users/ beneficiaries.

We recommended to the management the following courses of action: 

The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer immediately demand from suppliers a valid receipts/invoices. 

To inform the nearest office of the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) the suppliers/contractors who do not issue Sales Invoices or non-compliant to tax rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer to discontinue the practice of charging prior year’s expenditure to current year appropriation and that proper matching of expenses against appropriation should always be observed pursuant to the above-cited regulations.

We recommended Management to ensure that all expenses are duly authorized, adequately funded and documented; and to allow expenditures only within the limit of the appropriation pursuant to the provisions of PD No. 1177. 

We further recommended Management to refrain from journalizing the Monetized Leave credits of Employees to Terminal Leave Benefits and use appropriate account to record the expense.

We recommended the management to strictly adhere to Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V

 of EO 292 on the guidelines on Monetization of Leave Credits and documentations set forth by COA Circular 2012-001 to avoid improper payment of leave credits.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to enjoin all employees of the municipality to comply with the requirements set forth under Section 47 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act and adopt control measures to ensure that the employee’s monthly net take home pay must not be lower than Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00) after deducting mandatory contributions and other obligations.

We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report approved by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We reiterated that the Management require the concerned employees to submit copies of contracts, purchase orders, delivery receipts and all necessary documents within the deadline set by COA Circular No. 2009-001 to permit the Audit Team to conduct timely review of the contracts and to prevent the penalty to be imposed in case of unjustified failure on the part of the official or employee concerned to submit the documents as required.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant see to it that supporting documents are complete before certifying the Disbursement Voucher and effecting payment. Strict compliance with the provisions of the law, rules and regulations pertaining to documentation be always adhered for all claims against government funds.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to identify the nature of the reconciling items and adjust their records to properly reflect the Cash in Bank – LCCA account in the financial statements of the Municipality.
The following is the suggested pro-forma adjusting entry:
For Check cancellation:
Cash in Bank-LCCA			xx
Accounts Payable				xx
For Replacement:

Cash in Bank-LCCA			xx

We have recommended that the Management instruct the Accounting Unit to adjust the RPT/SET Receivables to include the amount written off at year-end to present fairly the balances of these accounts in the financial statements pursuant to Section 246 of the Local Government Code and Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I.

We have recommended that the Head of the Agency create Inventory Committee to conduct and complete the physical inventory and warrant full compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the existence, condition and accountability assertion of the Municipality on Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) in the Financial Statements.

We have reiterated that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be complied. Thus, we recommend Management to require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the General Services Officer in coordination with the Municipal Engineer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant to record inventory using the prescribed perpetual method of accounting on all purchased supplies and use inventory tags or stock cards for monitoring. to stop the practice of using the Petty Cash Fund for recurring and non-emergency expenses as mandated by COA Circular No. 97-002;
to stop the practice of procuring supplies and materials by piecemeal through shopping to attain cost savings efficiency in terms of processing, inspection, acceptance and payment; and

Recommendation:
With the observed lapses, on payment of Consultancy services that did not go through the procurement process, insufficient documentation and not highly technical in nature contrary to the 2016 Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act No. 9184 and item 9.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001,  we have recommended the following:
The Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to procure consultancy services pursuant to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184
The Municipality engage the services of consultants only for those projects or activities of the government which is highly technical in nature or those requiring adequate external technical and professional expertise that are beyond the capability and/or capacity of the government to undertake such as, but not limited to: a.) advisory and review services b.) pre-investment or feasibility studies c.) design d.) construction supervision e.) management and related services f.) other technical services or special studies
The  Municipality ensure that the functions of consultants are not a duplication of the functions of the existing personnel of the agency to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary expenses
The Accountant ensure the completeness of documents required under 9.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 prior to payment of claims for consultancy services

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the management to strictly adhere to the provisions of the Manual on New Government Accounting System in keeping and managing of government properties. They should conduct physical inventory of government properties regularly and prepare and update the required reports needed to aid in verifying the PPE balances and to determine whether the PPE is serviceable or unserviceable. They should also maintain subsidiary records and Property Card to facilitate periodic reconciliation of the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment with the books of accounts. We have also recommended the management to recognize the utilized portion of on-going infrastructure projects thru the Construction-in-Progress Account which form part of Property, Plant, and Equipment.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive should require the Bids and Award Committee together with the BAC Secretariat and the Technical Working Group Members to strictly observe and adhere to all the provisions of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act 9184 on all their procurement transactions.
Also, they should invite in writing, at least three observers from Commission on Audit, from duly recognized private group, and from Non-government Organization in all stages of the procurement process to enhance transparency of the process.
We have also recommended that copies of all contracts, preparatory requirements, and their supporting documents of procurement activities should be submitted as separate set of documents to our office within five (5) working days upon perfection of the contract in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We have recommended that the authorizing officer strictly adhere to the above-cited provisions in granting cash advances. We have also recommended that all officers or employees liquidate their cash advances within the periods cited in item 1.2 in COA Circular 2012-001.
If not liquidated within the specified period, we have recommended that the agency withhold the salaries and/or other benefits of the concerned officers or employees as provided for under Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and/or, file appropriate administrative and criminal actions against the officials or employees if necessary, and no granting of additional cash advances to officials or employees unless their previous cash advances were liquidated and limit the grant of cash advance to elected officials for their travelling expenses and allowances only.
We have also recommended the accounting unit to rectify the balance of “Due from Officers and Employees” by transferring the balances which are more appropriately recorded to the account “Advances for Officers and Employees”.

We have recommended that the Accounting unit verify the journal entries to be recorded to the journal entry vouchers. Since the GSO designation was abolished, we then have recommended the management to notify the designated Supply Officer for all its government purchases; and the Supply Officer to maintain stock cards and property cards, and reconcile his/her records with the records maintained by the Accounting Unit to make the necessary adjustments if any.

We have recommended that the Accountant strictly observe that all claims against government funds be supported with complete documentation and that all documents/forms forming part thereof be properly accomplished in accordance with accounting and auditing rules and regulations. We have further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer pay only claims that are duly approved/certified and supported with complete documentation as required under Section 4(6) of PD 1445. Also, we have recommended the agency to avoid reimbursement as much as possible.

We have recommended that all officials receiving transportation allowance, to refrain from reimbursing gasoline expenses for travels within fifty (50) km from their official stations in adherence with 3.13, Annex “B” of COA Circular No. 2012-003 and Section 8 of Article IX(B) of the Constitution.

We have recommended the management to monitor the transactions affecting the reciprocal accounts, and to rectify the incorrect adjustment made to the “Due to Other Funds” account of Trust Fund. We have also recommended the management to scrutinize any other reconciling items that made the reciprocal accounts not balanced. Likewise, periodic reconciliation of accounts should be made to ensure accuracy of the recorded transactions.

We have recommended the Sangguniang Bayan Members, thru their Secretary, to submit monthly the actual attendance   of the regular sessions to the Human Resource Management Office and to accomplish application for leave if applicable, for recording in leave cards, and compute the proportionate reduction thereto in case of unauthorized absences in accordance with Section 7.5 of LBC No. 103.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to enjoined all employees of the municipality to comply with the requirements set forth under Section 52 of the General Appropriations Act and adopt control measures to ensure that the employee’s monthly net take home pay not be lower than Three Thousand Five Hundred Pesos (P 3,500.00) after deducting mandatory contributions and other obligations.

We have recommended the management to refrain from granting allowance to the COMELEC Official, and other national government officials not expressly enumerated under 2.0 of the Compensation Policy Guidelines No. 98-1 to avoid the incurrence of that kind of irregular expenditure.

We have recommended the management to demand for the accomplishment reports of the Consultants, and to review the necessity of their services. We have also recommended the management in agreement with the Consultants, to replace the redundant functions of the consultants as against the functions of regular employees.

We have recommended that the agency utilize the Special Education Fund solely for the purposes that the fund was established in accordance with the provisions of Section 272 of Republic Act No. 7160 and No. 4, Joint Circular No. 01  s. 1998 of DECS, DBM and DILG to carry out and maximize its intended benefits.
We have recommended that the agency implement the ATM Payroll System to avail its advantages, and consequently eliminating the disadvantages of manual system.
